title: bytesPushed recorded BEFORE the chunk is pushed so every curve point is offset by one chunk size
description: In measureConvergenceProfile, bytesPushed is incremented BEFORE decoder.push(chunk) is called (line 366 then 367). The 'progress' event fires after the push completes, and at that moment the code records curve points using bytesPushed, which already includes the bytes from the chunk that just caused the progress event. The intent is to record 'how many bytes were needed to reach this quality', but because bytesPushed is pre-incremented, the recorded value is already one full chunk (32 KB) ahead. The real convergence byte offset in the manifest will be inflated by up to CHUNK (32768) bytes. Compare with backends.ts line 366-367: bytesPushed += chunk.length followed by await decoder.push(chunk).
evidence: Code at backends.ts:366-367 shows `bytesPushed += chunk.length` executed before `await Promise.resolve(decoder.push(chunk))`. The progress event fires inside the decoder after the push, so it reads bytesPushed which already includes the current chunk — recording the convergence byte offset one chunk (up to 32768 bytes) too high.

title: dispose() called twice in the finally path when an exception is thrown during push/close
description: In measureConvergenceProfile, the try/catch around the push loop catches exceptions, calls dispose() via the catch block (line 372), and returns undefined. Then the outer finally block (line 374) calls dispose() again unconditionally. The dispose() call in the catch is redundant because the finally always runs after both the try and the catch. For most decoder implementations this is a no-op double-free, but if dispose() is not idempotent (e.g., the WASM decoder frees native memory), this is a double-free bug that can corrupt state or throw. The finally-only pattern used in decodeFinal (line 261) is correct; the catch+finally pattern here is not.
evidence: Code at backends.ts:371-376 shows dispose() called in both the catch block and the finally block. The finally always runs after catch, so on exception paths dispose is called twice. If the WASM decoder is not idempotent on double-dispose, this is a double-free; the correct pattern (used in decodeFinal) is dispose only in finally.

title: planShard returns all items when n<=0 instead of throwing, but tests expect a throw for n=0
description: planShard(items, i, 0) returns a full copy of items via items.slice() because the first guard is `if (n <= 0) return items.slice()`. However the shard.test.ts test at line 19 asserts `expect(() => planShard(['a'], 0, 0)).toThrow()`. The implementation returns silently rather than throwing. This means the test cannot pass as written, and more importantly any caller that passes n=0 expecting an error gets back all items, silently creating an unintended full-batch shard instead of failing.
evidence: shard.ts:28 returns `items.slice()` for n<=0; shard.test.ts:18-20 asserts `expect(() => planShard(['a'], 2, 2)).toThrow()`, `expect(() => planShard(['a'], -1, 2)).toThrow()`, and `expect(() => planShard(['a'], 0, 0)).toThrow()`. The implementation never throws for any of these cases — it silently returns [] or items.slice() — so all three test assertions will fail.

title: tiling-ingest tests call non-async imageIdForPath without await and treat ingestImage result as a string
description: In the tiling-ingest.test.ts at lines 56-58, `imageIdForPath(master)` is called without await — imageIdForPath returns a Promise<string> (it is async), so imageId will be a Promise object rather than a string. The subsequent readFile(join(out, 'images', imageId, 'manifest.json')) will produce a path containing '[object Promise]', causing the readFile to fail with ENOENT. Additionally on line 56, `expect(await ingestImage(master, backends, { outDir: out })).toBe('written')` directly compares the IngestResult object (not .outcome) to the string 'written', which will always fail.
evidence: hash.ts:24 shows `imageIdForPath` is declared `async` returning `Promise<string>`. tiling-ingest.test.ts:58 calls it without await, so imageId is a Promise object. IngestResult is a typed object with an `outcome` field, so `.toBe('written')` on the whole object always fails. Both bugs are in the same test at lines 56 and 58.

title: Second tiling test also calls imageIdForPath without await, and checks full.tiled===false which contradicts the Phase 3 all-JXTC design
description: In the second tiling test ('standard RAW ingest keeps whole-frame full level'), imageIdForPath(master) is again called without await (line 84), producing a Promise-as-path bug identical to logic-4. Additionally the test expects `full.tiled === false` for a standard 1280x960 RAW master (line 90), but we need to verify whether the Phase-3 ladder routes all levels through encodeTileContainer.
evidence: tiling-ingest.test.ts:84 has the same missing-await bug as logic-4. The `full.tiled===false` assertion at line 90 may or may not be correct per current ladder behavior, but the test is definitively broken by the missing await making all manifest assertions moot regardless.

title: totalStagedBytes accumulates from ALL completed items including prior-run checkpoint entries, not just this-run written ones
description: The totalStagedBytes reduction at line 756 (in-process path) and line 926 (worker path) sums cpState.completed, which includes entries loaded from a prior checkpoint run when --resume is used. The B10 comment explicitly says 'perImage should reflect only this run's activity', but that fix is applied only to perImage. totalStagedBytes continues to be computed from the full cpState.completed array, inflating the reported figure to include prior-run bytes.
code_excerpt:
const s = cpState.completed.reduce((sum: number, c: any) => sum + (c.stagedBytes || 0), 0);
    if (s > 0) result.totalStagedBytes = s;
evidence: ingest.ts:750-757 shows `perImage` is filtered with `thisRun` Set (B10 fix) but the `totalStagedBytes` reduce at line 756 operates on the unfiltered `cpState.completed`. The same pattern appears in the worker path at line 926. The comment at line 750 documents the intent but the fix was only partially applied.

title: buildManifest sorts levels by pixel count (w*h) but toEntry uses long-edge for levelSize — a non-square level sorted by area differs from long-edge order
description: buildManifest sorts by `a.w * a.h - b.w * b.h` (area ascending). The rest of the pipeline treats levels as ascending by long edge. For non-square levels with different aspect ratios, area order and long-edge order can diverge, causing buildIndexEntry which uses levels[0] as the thumbnail to pick the wrong level.
code_excerpt: const levels = [...args.levels].sort((a, b) => a.w * a.h - b.w * b.h);
evidence: manifest.ts:54 confirms area sort. In practice RAW images from the supported cameras (ORF/DNG/CR2) maintain consistent aspect ratios across levels since they downscale proportionally, so area and long-edge order would agree for typical inputs. A divergence requires two levels with very different aspect ratios, which does not occur in normal pyramid ladders. The risk is real for unusual inputs but unlikely to manifest in practice.

title: JPEG SOF parser bounds check uses i+8 but reads bytes[i+8], risking out-of-bounds on last-byte-of-buffer SOF markers
description: The getJpegDimensions loop guard is `while (i + 8 < bytes.length)`. If a SOF marker is at exactly the last 9 bytes of the buffer (i+8 == bytes.length-1), the guard passes and all reads are safe. The potential infinite spin occurs only when a zero-length segment (len=2) is encountered — i advances by only 2, and if the next two bytes are also 0x00, another len=2 is computed, causing slow iteration without reading past the end.
evidence: ingest.ts:174 computes `len = ((bytes[i+2]<<8)|bytes[i+3])+2`. If the segment length field is 0x0000, len=2 and i advances by 2 to what may still be a non-0xff byte, causing i++ to step 1 byte, and another potential len=2 next iteration — the loop degrades to O(N) scan on pathological data. This is a real but low-severity issue for corrupt JPEG inputs only.

title: extractBasicMetadata passes !opts.stripGps which means GPS is INCLUDED by default, inverting the privacy intent of stripGps
description: The finder self-corrected: when stripGps=true, gps=false (GPS not extracted) — correct. When stripGps=false or undefined, gps=true (GPS extracted) — also correct. The inversion in the parameter name is intentional and the logic is sound.
code_excerpt: const meta = await extractBasicMetadata(bytes, !opts.stripGps);
evidence: The finder correctly self-analyzed this as not a bug: `!opts.stripGps` correctly passes `gps=true` when stripGps is falsy (include GPS) and `gps=false` when stripGps is true (exclude GPS). The logic is correct — the double-negative in the parameter name is slightly confusing but not a defect.

title: pMapLimit has a redundant cur>=items.length guard inside the runner, but the real race is an unsynchronized i++ that can cause two runners to process the same index
description: pMapLimit uses a shared `let i = 0` counter incremented with `const cur = i++`. In JavaScript this is safe because the event loop is single-threaded. The fn(items[cur]).catch(()=>{}) silently swallows all errors — callers have no way to detect partial failures.
evidence: pMapLimit at ingest.ts:1053-1062 swallows all per-item errors with `.catch(()=>{})`. Callers like rebuildIndex and removeOrphans receive no signal when a manifest parse fails or a file I/O error occurs mid-scan. The redundant `cur >= items.length` guard is a minor style issue, but the silent error swallowing is the real correctness gap.

title: measureConvergenceProfile skips small images (<1024 long edge) but the guard uses Math.max which is correct — however returns undefined for zero-dimension decode
description: When decodeFinal returns a frame with w=0, h=0, the 0<1024 guard fires silently. The calling code in attachConverged has its own guard, so the profile is dropped with no telemetry signal.
evidence: The guard at backends.ts:288 is correct for the documented behavior. The silent drop when w=0/h=0 is defense-in-depth that could hide a decoder bug, but it requires an unusual condition (decodeFinal returning zero dimensions) that would only occur if the WASM decoder is broken. The caller's own size guard makes this a double-safety rather than a gap.

title: encodeBigLevelsRgba16 order-violation check uses > not >= for dst vs cur, allowing equal-size levels to bypass and re-encode the same resolution
description: The order check at line 70 is `if (dst.w > curW || dst.h > curH)`, meaning equal dimensions pass through without triggering the error. The code then checks `if (dst.w !== curW || dst.h !== curH)` before downscaling, which correctly skips downscaling for the same dimensions. But two targets producing the same dst due to integer rounding would produce a duplicate level.
evidence: rgb16.ts:70 uses strict `>` so equal dimensions pass the guard silently. The subsequent dst.w !== curW check at line 73 skips downscaling but still proceeds to encode the same buffer again, producing a duplicate level entry in the returned array. The ladder.ts dedup (lastW16/lastH16) prevents this in the main build path, but encodeBigLevelsRgba16 called standalone has no such dedup.

title: acceptUnsupported default logic: `opts.acceptUnsupported !== false` treats undefined as true, but the CLI default is true via Zod — the double-negative is fragile for programmatic callers
description: The acceptUnsupported default in ingestImage is `const accept = opts.acceptUnsupported !== false`. This means undefined → true (accept). For programmatic callers who omit the field expecting strict mode, behavior is silently accept-degraded.
code_excerpt: const accept = opts.acceptUnsupported !== false; // Q2 default accept-degraded
evidence: The comment '// Q2 default accept-degraded' documents this as intentional behavior. The IngestOptions interface marks it optional with the expected default of true. The double-negative is equivalent to `opts.acceptUnsupported ?? true` and is a documented design choice, not a bug.

title: IngestResult.outcome is 'written' even for dry-run (misleads callers; plan is returned but outcome should be 'dry-run')
description: When dryRun is true, ingestImage returns `{ outcome: 'written', plan: plan! }`. The outcome 'written' is factually wrong for a dry-run since nothing was actually written to disk. BatchResult.written is incremented for these, inflating the written count.
code_excerpt: return { outcome: "written", plan: plan!, degraded: usedFallback || undefined };
evidence: ingest.ts:503 returns `outcome: 'written'` in the dryRun branch. ingest.ts:724 checks `if (outcome === 'written') { result.written++ }` unconditionally, so dry-run runs inflate the written counter. The IngestOutcome type at line 41 only has 'written' | 'skipped', confirming no dry-run outcome variant exists.

category: no property-based testing on math/state-heavy code
title: planLadder filter uses s < masterLong (strict) so a master whose long edge exactly equals a LEVEL_SIZE gets no sidecar for that size
description: planLadder filters `LEVEL_SIZES.filter((s) => s < masterLong && masterLong / s >= NEAR_FULL_RATIO)`. For a master with exactly 1024 long edge, the 1024 sidecar is excluded. This is correct semantics but lacks a boundary test.
code_excerpt: sizes = LEVEL_SIZES.filter((s) => s < masterLong && masterLong / s >= NEAR_FULL_RATIO);
evidence: quality.ts:52 uses strict `<` which correctly excludes same-size sidecars, but the NEAR_FULL_RATIO check is vacuous when s approaches masterLong. No test covers the exact-boundary case. This is a legitimate opportunity to add a boundary regression test to prevent LEVEL_SIZES changes from silently altering sidecar generation.

title: progress telemetry uses idx+1 but idx is already the next file index (post-increment), so progress shows 'done+2/total' on first item
description: In the in-process batch path, `const idx = next++` makes idx the 0-based index of the current file. `tel?.progress(idx + 1, total, path)` then reports 1-based progress correctly. The worker pool path uses the same pattern.
evidence: ingest.ts:686-710 shows `const idx = next++` followed by `tel?.progress(idx + 1, total, path)`. This correctly reports 1-based progress (file 0 → reports 1/N, file 1 → reports 2/N). The concern about idx exceeding activeFiles.length is guarded by the `if (idx >= activeFiles.length) return` check on line 687, which fires before progress is called. JavaScript's single-threaded event loop means the captured `idx` value is always the one that was incremented for this coroutine.

title: Config file path accepted from CLI and read without any path restriction, enabling read of arbitrary files
description: The --config flag accepts any string and passes it directly to readFile without validating that the path is within an expected directory.
evidence: This is a local CLI tool for a developer/biodiversity platform — the config flag is a standard feature of CLI tools and reading files specified by the operator is expected behavior, not an attack surface. The finder's threat model (attacker controls CLI arguments) does not apply to a local ingest tool where the operator controls the invocation.

title: --rm accepts arbitrary imageId string and joins it directly into filesystem paths without sanitization
description: The rm subcommand accepts a target from CLI and joins it into paths. A value without separators/dots is used directly as imageId in path joins, so a crafted value like '../../etc' that somehow lacks those characters could escape outDir.
evidence: cli.ts:301 checks for path separators and dots to route to imageIdForPath, but an imageId passed directly (without separators/dots) is used unvalidated in join(outDir, 'images', imageId, '.lock') at lock.ts:173. A crafted string like '..%2fetc' or a hex-looking string with embedded escapes would not be caught. The imageId regex /^[0-9a-f]{16}$/ is used for explain but not for rm. Adding this validation at the rm path entry point closes the gap.

title: Level contenthash from manifest used in filesystem path without validation against expected hex format
description: Each lv.contenthash is joined directly into a filesystem path as join(levelsDir, lv.contenthash + '.jxl'). The schema enforces z.string().length(16) but not hex-only characters.
code_excerpt: const lp = join(levelsDir, `${lv.contenthash}.jxl`);
evidence: schema.ts:45 shows `contenthash: z.string().length(16)` with no regex constraint. A crafted manifest with contenthash containing path separators (e.g. '../../../x') is length-16 compliant and would pass schema validation, then be joined into a filesystem path in validate.ts:74 and ingest.ts writeLevelFiles. Adding `.regex(/^[0-9a-f]{16}$/)` to the Zod schema closes the gap at parse time for all callers.

title: contenthash used to construct level file paths without hex-character validation
description: writeLevelFiles joins entry.contenthash directly into the levelsDir path without checking that the hash contains only hex characters.
code_excerpt: const dest = join(levelsDir, `${entry.contenthash}.jxl`);
evidence: Same root cause as security-3: the Zod schema for contenthash only validates length(16), not character set. Fixing security-3 (adding regex to levelEntrySchema) would also fix this finding since both use parseManifest to validate incoming manifests. This finding is a duplicate instance of the same gap.

title: imageId used as path component in acquireImageWriteLock without hex validation
description: acquireImageWriteLock constructs the lock path as join(outDir, 'images', imageId, '.lock') without validating imageId against /^[0-9a-f]{16}$/.
evidence: lock.ts constructs a path from imageId without validation. Callers that pass user-controlled imageId (e.g. the rm subcommand at cli.ts:304) could escape the gallery directory. The fix belongs at the CLI entry point where imageId is first accepted from user input, adding a regex guard before any path operations.

title: 64-bit truncated SHA-256 used as content-addressed storage key risks birthday collision at ~2B objects
description: contentHash and imageIdForPath both use only the first 16 hex characters (64 bits) of SHA-256.
code_excerpt: const HASH_TRUNC_BYTES = 8; // 16 hex chars = 64-bit
evidence: hash.ts:5 confirms 64-bit truncation. The code comment at line 15-19 already documents the birthday collision risk and mentions a future migration to 96-bit. For a biodiversity platform ingesting millions of images over years, the ~2^32 collision boundary is a real operational concern that is documented but not yet addressed.

title: Full CLI argv (including sensitive flags and paths) written to world-readable runlog file
description: The runlog record includes args: process.argv.slice(2), capturing the complete command line including --out path, --config path, and any other flags.
code_excerpt: args: process.argv.slice(2),
evidence: cli.ts:461 confirms `args: process.argv.slice(2)` is written into the runlog. For a local tool this is low risk, but flags like --config pointing to sensitive paths or environment-specific paths would be exposed to any user with read access to the output directory.

title: GPS coordinates extracted and stored in manifest metadata by default; opt-out is not the default
description: extractBasicMetadata is called with gps = !opts.stripGps, meaning GPS data is included by default. There is no --strip-gps CLI flag exposed to users.
code_excerpt: const meta = await extractBasicMetadata(bytes, !opts.stripGps);
evidence: ingest.ts:465 confirms GPS is included by default. The CLI does not expose a --strip-gps flag. For a biodiversity platform documenting sensitive species locations (explicitly mentioned in CLAUDE.md), GPS-in-by-default with no easy opt-out is a real privacy concern that should at minimum expose a --strip-gps CLI flag.

title: 512 MB master file size limit is checked only after reading the file into memory
description: MAX_MASTER_BYTES = 512 * 1024 * 1024 is checked against info.size (from stat) before the readFile call, so a TOCTOU race or symlink could bypass it.
evidence: The size check at ingest.ts against `info.size` (from stat) happens before the readFile. A file that grows between stat and read is an edge case on local filesystems, and the tool is a local ingest CLI, not a server processing untrusted uploads. The TOCTOU concern is theoretical for normal usage patterns.

title: contenthash field in levelEntrySchema should enforce hex-only character set, not just length
description: levelEntrySchema uses z.string().length(16) for contenthash but does not add a regex constraint for [0-9a-f]{16}.
code_excerpt: contenthash: z.string().length(16),
evidence: schema.ts:45 confirms `z.string().length(16)` with no hex regex. This is the root-cause fix for security-3 and security-4. Adding `.regex(/^[0-9a-f]{16}$/)` is a one-line change that closes path traversal via crafted manifests at the schema boundary, consistent with the imageId validation at line 61.

title: master.name field has no length or character-set constraint, allowing oversized or control-character filenames in manifests
description: masterInfoSchema validates format and mtimeMs but places no constraint on name beyond being a non-empty string.
code_excerpt: name: z.string(),
evidence: schema.ts:52 shows `name: z.string()` with no length or character constraints. The name is printed in the explain subcommand (potential terminal injection via ANSI escapes) but is not used in filesystem operations. The risk is low for a local tool but adding `.max(255).regex(/^[^\u0000-\u001f]+$/)` would be a safe defensive improvement.

title: exifr is dynamically imported with no version pinning enforcement or integrity check
description: exifr is loaded via dynamic import with a catch-null fallback processing attacker-controlled binary files.
code_excerpt: const exifrMod: any = await import("exifr").catch(() => null);
evidence: This is a supply-chain concern that applies to any npm dependency, not specific to this code pattern. The dynamic import with catch-null is the correct pattern for optional dependencies. Whether bun.lock pins exact versions is not verifiable from the source files alone. This is a valid CI/process concern but not a code defect.

title: In-process batch: `next++` counter is read/incremented between awaits, creating a TOCTOU window
description: In the in-process batch path, `let next = 0` is shared across coroutines. Each coroutine does `const idx = next++` then awaits. Because JS is single-threaded the increment is safe.
evidence: JavaScript's single-threaded event loop guarantees that `const idx = next++` is atomic — no other coroutine can run between the read and the increment. The pattern is correct and is a standard JS async concurrency idiom. The concern about copy-paste to threaded contexts is a speculative future risk, not a current bug.

title: Timeout race: the losing `workP` promise is detached and can write files after the function returns
description: When --timeout-ms fires, Promise.race rejects and workP is detached via workP.catch(()=>{}). workP continues running in the background, potentially writing level files and renaming the manifest after the caller has marked the image as failed and released the write lock.
evidence: ingest.ts:514-522 confirms workP is detached on timeout. The workP continues to applyIngestPlan which calls writeLevelFiles and renameManifest. The caller's batch loop receives the timeout error, marks the image as failed, and may release the per-image write lock — while the detached workP still holds references to the same image directory and continues mutating it.

category: lock ordering / missing lock on abort path
title: Abort handler terminates workers and rejects pending promises without releasing per-image write locks
description: The onAbort signal handler calls w.terminate() on all workers and rejects pending promises. Per-image write locks held by dispatcher coroutines are released in each coroutine's finally block, but a race between imgLock acquisition and pending map registration means some locks may never be released.
evidence: ingest.ts:838-908 shows the dispatcher acquires imgLock at line ~856, then creates the pending Promise and registers it. If onAbort fires between imgLock acquisition and pending.set(), the coroutine breaks at `if (dead.has(wi)) break` without entering the finally block that releases imgLock. The lock file then persists as stale until the 24h timeout or dead-PID detection.

title: Checkpoint state (`cpState`, `inFlight`, `result`) is mutated by multiple concurrent coroutines without serialization
description: Both in-process and worker-pool paths concurrently mutate cpState.completed, cpState.failed, inFlight, result.written etc. The checkpoint written to disk may transiently omit in-flight paths.
evidence: JavaScript's event loop serializes all microtask execution — concurrent async coroutines interleave only at await points, not truly in parallel. Mutations to cpState, inFlight, and result between awaits are always atomic from the JS runtime's perspective. The checkpoint snapshot taken in doPersist is a snapshot of state at a single event-loop tick, which is consistent. This is not a real race condition.

category: TOCTOU / missing lock during GC scan
title: GC orphan scan has a TOCTOU gap between readdir(levelsDir) and the per-file stat/unlink
description: Between readdir and unlink, a concurrent ingest may have written a level file that is not yet in any manifest but was written after the readdir snapshot. The 10-minute GRACE_MS window mitigates this.
evidence: The GRACE_MS=600000ms window correctly handles normal cases, but relies on a level file being written at most 10 minutes before GC runs. If a level file is written by an ingest that started just over 10 minutes ago, was written to disk, but the manifest rename hasn't completed yet, it could be falsely identified as orphan. The gallery write lock would prevent this under normal operation, but advisory locking is not enforced across all write paths.

title: WASM singleton init: `initialized` flag set after `await init(...)` but `initPromise` checked before
description: Two concurrent callers could both observe `initPromise === null` and both assign a new IIFE, double-initializing the WASM module.
evidence: The assignment `initPromise = (async () => {...})()` is synchronous — it creates and assigns the Promise before yielding. In JavaScript's single-threaded event loop, no other code can execute between `if (!initPromise)` and `initPromise = ...`. The second concurrent caller will see `initPromise !== null` at its next check (after the first await). The comment 'singleton guard prevents double wasm read+init under any interleaving' is correct.

title: Module-level `cachedSsim` SSIM lazy-init has the same double-call race as raw-backend ensureWasm
description: Two measureConvergenceProfile calls running concurrently can both observe `cachedSsim === undefined` and both await import('ssim.js').
evidence: Unlike conc-6 (where initPromise is assigned synchronously before any await), cachedSsim is assigned only after `await import('ssim.js')` completes. Two concurrent callers both enter the `if (cachedSsim !== undefined) return` guard as undefined, both proceed to await import, and both assign cachedSsim — resulting in two dynamic import invocations. Node deduplicates ESM module evaluation so this is not a correctness bug, but it is redundant work. A pending-promise pattern (like conc-6's initPromise) would fix it.

title: `rm` subcommand acquires per-image lock then gallery write lock, while `batch` acquires gallery lock then per-image lock — potential lock-order deadlock
description: rm path: image lock first (line 304), then gallery lock (line 315). Batch path: gallery lock first (line 415), then per-image locks inside ingestBatch. This is a classic A-then-B vs B-then-A lock ordering inversion.
evidence: cli.ts:304 acquires image lock before gallery lock at line 315. cli.ts:415 acquires gallery lock before any image locks. If `rm` holds the image lock and is waiting for the gallery lock, while `batch` holds the gallery lock and is waiting for the same image lock, both will time out after 30s — a de facto timeout deadlock. The fix is to have rm acquire the gallery lock first or release the image lock before acquiring the gallery lock.

title: `scheduleCompletedPersist` setTimeout callback's async errors leave `completedDirty=false` with data not actually persisted
description: completedDirty is set to false before `await doPersist`. If doPersist fails after the flag is cleared, subsequent scheduleCompletedPersist calls won't reschedule, leaving the checkpoint out of sync.
evidence: ingest.ts:664-669 confirms completedDirty is set false before the await. The `.catch(() => {})` on doPersist suppresses the error but does not reset completedDirty to true, so a failed persist leaves the dirty flag in a cleared state. The checkpoint may then miss completed entries until the next forceFlushCheckpoint call on a success or failure path.

title: `applyIngestPlan` reads the `levels/` directory once and caches it, but concurrent writers may add files between the snapshot and level writes
description: readdir(levelsDir) is called once to build existing set. If another ingest writes a level with the same contenthash between snapshot and skip-check, both attempt writeFileAtomic. EEXIST is handled correctly for content-addressed files.
evidence: ingest.ts shows the EEXIST handler in writeFileAtomic correctly handles concurrent writes to content-addressed files — the second write is safely skipped. The stale-snapshot risk (GC deleting a file after readdir shows it as existing) is guarded by the GRACE_MS window in removeOrphans. This is correctly designed defense-in-depth, not a gap.

title: Runlog append read-modify-write is not protected by the gallery write lock for sharded runs
description: The runlog file is read, modified, and rewritten. For sharded runs (multiple CLI processes), each holds its own gallery write lock but the runlog write is not skipped — two shard processes can race to read-modify-write the same runlog file.
evidence: cli.ts:453 shows the runlog write is gated by `if (!parsed.shard && !dryRun)` — it is skipped for sharded runs. The finding's claim that 'sharded runs do not skip the runlog write' is incorrect; the guard explicitly excludes sharded runs. For single-process runs the gallery write lock is held during the runlog write. Not a real race.

title: Opportunity: `AbortSignal` is not threaded into `workP` / `ingestImage` so timeout cancellation cannot interrupt decode/encode
description: When timeout-ms fires, workP continues to run decode/encode operations with no way to interrupt them.
evidence: This is a genuine capability gap: conc-2 shows the detached workP continues running, and without an AbortSignal threaded into the WASM decode/encode calls there is no mechanism to interrupt it. The opportunity is real but complex to implement since WASM push() is synchronous and cannot be interrupted mid-execution per CLAUDE.md invariants.

title: Opportunity: concurrent `forceFlushCheckpoint` calls from multiple coroutines can issue overlapping writes
description: forceFlushCheckpoint has no mutex, so concurrent error-path callers both call doPersist with potentially different inFlight snapshots.
evidence: forceFlushCheckpoint has no serialization guard. Two concurrent callers both call doPersist -> writeCheckpoint -> writeFileAtomic. The atomic rename means only one wins, but the losing write's snapshot (which may be the more recent state) is discarded. In practice this only matters if two workers fail concurrently at exactly the same moment, so impact is low but the opportunity to add a pending-write guard is real.

category: lock ordering / write-lock acquired inside read-lock wait
title: Read-lock acquisition re-checks write lock after claiming read lock but does not re-release before timeout check, creating a retry storm
description: In acquireReadLockFile, step 3 checks if a writer raced. If a writer is present, the read lock is released and the loop continues. The timeout check after unlink could fire immediately after releasing the lock.
evidence: The timeout check at lock.ts:148 only fires if `Date.now() - start > timeoutMs`, which is a strict greater-than. The pattern correctly releases the read lock before checking timeout, avoiding a deadlock. The O(readers) drain cost per poll cycle is a performance concern (already captured in errors-17) rather than a lock correctness bug.

title: pMapLimit swallows all per-item errors unconditionally with .catch(() => {})
description: The exported pMapLimit utility catches and discards every error thrown by the callback fn.
code_excerpt: await fn(items[cur]).catch(() => {});
evidence: Duplicate of logic-10. pMapLimit at ingest.ts:1059 confirms `.catch(() => {})`. This is a real issue — callers like rebuildIndex and removeOrphans cannot distinguish silent failures from successes. The fix would either propagate errors or accept an optional onError callback.

title: getCachedSsimFn outer catch swallows the exception type, collapsing import errors with logic errors
description: The outer try/catch sets cachedSsim = null for any thrown value, including non-import exceptions like TypeError from bad property access.
evidence: backends.ts:220-228 shows the outer catch swallows any exception from the `f = ssimMod && (ssimMod.default || ssimMod).ssim` property access chain. A TypeError here would set cachedSsim=null permanently, silently degrading all convergence profiling to no-SSIM mode. The inner `.catch(() => null)` already handles import failure; the outer catch masks programming errors.

title: measureConvergenceProfile calls dispose() in both catch and finally, risking double-dispose of the decoder
description: Duplicate of logic-2. dispose() is called in both catch and finally.
evidence: Same code as logic-2. Confirmed duplicate finding. dispose() at backends.ts:372 in catch and again at 375 in finally — the finally always runs after catch so double-dispose is guaranteed on exception paths.

category: unchecked return value / missing null check
title: Test calls imageIdForPath synchronously (no await) — always receives a Promise as imageId
description: imageIdForPath is async. Called without await at line 60, so imageId is a Promise object, making all path operations use '[object Promise]' as a directory name.
code_excerpt: const imageId = imageIdForPath(master);
evidence: Duplicate of logic-4. hash.ts:24 confirms imageIdForPath is async. tiling-ingest.test.ts:58 calls it without await. Same finding, confirmed.

category: unchecked return value / missing null check
title: Second test also calls imageIdForPath without await — same Promise-as-path bug
description: Same as errors-4 but in the second test at line 84.
code_excerpt: const imageId = imageIdForPath(master);
evidence: Duplicate of logic-5. tiling-ingest.test.ts:84 confirmed without await. Same defect.

title: ingestImage return type is IngestResult but test asserts it equals the string 'written'
description: ingestImage returns Promise<IngestResult> (object with outcome field). Test does expect(await ingestImage(...)).toBe('written') comparing object to string — always fails.
code_excerpt: expect(await ingestImage(master, backends, { outDir: out })).toBe("written");
evidence: ingest.ts:41-49 defines IngestResult as an object with outcome, stagedBytes, plan, degraded fields. tiling-ingest.test.ts:56 and :82 compare the full IngestResult object to the string 'written' — this will always be false since the object reference != string 'written'. Should be `.outcome === 'written'`.

title: acquireWriteLockFile has no timeout on the waitForReadLocksToClear inner loop when a live reader refuses to release
description: The waitForReadLocksToClear deadline uses start + timeoutMs, which may leave very little budget for the drain phase after the wx-create loop.
code_excerpt: await waitForReadLocksToClear(lockPath, start + timeoutMs, label);
evidence: lock.ts:106 passes `start + timeoutMs` as the drain deadline. If the outer retry loop (stale-lock detection + sleep) consumes most of the timeout budget before wx-create succeeds, the drain phase inherits a near-zero remaining budget and immediately throws. This can produce spurious timeout errors under FS load or many concurrent readers.

title: Runlog append error is swallowed by empty outer catch, silently losing the run record
description: The entire runlog read-parse-push-write sequence is wrapped in try/catch {} with no logging.
code_excerpt: } catch {}
evidence: cli.ts:477 shows an empty `catch {}` swallowing all runlog write failures. ENOSPC, corrupt JSON, permission errors all result in silent loss of the run record. For a biodiversity platform audit trail this is a real correctness gap — at minimum a warning should be emitted to stderr.

title: rm subcommand does not release imgLock on error paths (before acquireWriteLock for rebuildIndex)
description: In the rm branch, if removeImage throws, imgLock.release() is never called because there is no try/finally around it.
evidence: cli.ts:304-319 shows imgLock acquired at line 304, removeImage awaited at line 307 (can throw), and imgLock.release() at line 319 with no try/finally wrapping the block. If removeImage throws, the function propagates the error without releasing imgLock, leaving a stale lock file until dead-PID detection or the 24h timeout.

title: buildManifest computes aspect = width / height without guarding height === 0
description: If a caller passes height: 0, aspect becomes Infinity. Zod's z.number().finite().positive() will catch this but the error message will be cryptic.
code_excerpt: aspect: round4(args.width / args.height),
evidence: manifest.ts:62 confirms the unguarded division. Zod validation at line 67 (`manifestSchema.parse(base)`) will catch Infinity via `.finite()` on the aspect field, but the error will be 'Invalid input: expected number, received number (Infinity)' rather than a clear 'height must be positive'. Adding an explicit guard before the division improves diagnosability.

title: Per-pass ssim and butteraugli computation errors are silently swallowed inside measureConvergenceProfile
description: Both ssimFn calls and comparator.compare calls are wrapped in bare catch {} blocks. If either throws, the pass is silently omitted from the curve.
evidence: backends.ts:331 and 336 show bare `catch {}` blocks for SSIM and Butteraugli computation. A programming error or type mismatch in these calls silently produces a qualityCurve with fewer points than expected. At minimum these should log a warning so operators can diagnose malformed convergence curves.

title: MAX_MASTER_BYTES size-guard error message exposes the full internal limit constant and actual file size in user-facing output
description: The error includes raw byte counts: `master too large: ${info.size} bytes (>${MAX_MASTER_BYTES})`.
code_excerpt: throw new Error(`master too large: ${info.size} bytes (>${MAX_MASTER_BYTES})`);
evidence: For a local CLI tool operated by the gallery owner, displaying the file size in bytes and the limit constant is helpful diagnostics, not information leakage. The tool is not a multi-tenant web service. This is a code style preference, not a security or correctness issue.

title: rebuildIndex does not handle ENOENT on the index rename — a concurrent delete of the tmp file causes silent data loss
description: In rebuildIndex, the tmp file is written then renamed. If a concurrent operation deletes the tmp file, rename gets ENOENT and throws.
code_excerpt:
await withEbusyRetry(() => writeFile(tmp, JSON.stringify(index, null, 2)), "write-index-tmp");
  await withEbusyRetry(() => rename(tmp, indexPath), "rename-index");
evidence: The tmp file is named with process.pid and a random suffix (based on patterns elsewhere in the codebase), making concurrent deletion extremely unlikely in normal operation. The gallery write lock is held during rebuildIndex, further reducing concurrent access. This is a theoretical edge case in adversarial environments, but not a realistic failure scenario for the tool's intended use.

title: No structured error taxonomy: all failures use plain Error, making programmatic error handling fragile
description: All error paths use plain Error with string messages. Callers must string-match to classify errors.
evidence: Genuine opportunity: the codebase uses plain Error throughout and callers differentiate by string matching (e.g. 'failed to acquire image write lock' at ingest.ts:701). A code property convention would enable reliable programmatic classification in --json mode and for retry/resume logic.

title: Per-image failure events emit message and code but not stack or error type, limiting post-mortem debugging
description: image-failed telemetry drops the stack trace, making WASM panic diagnosis require stderr scan.
code_excerpt: tel?.event?.("image-failed", { path, imageId, error: msg, code, durationMs: dur });
evidence: ingest.ts:739 confirms err.stack is not included in the image-failed event payload. For WASM panics ('unreachable executed'), the message alone provides no call stack context. This is a real observability gap for production debugging.

category: missing null check / unbounded input assumption
title: imageIdForPath is called inside a catch block with an empty catch body — errors during ID derivation for rm are silently ignored
description: If imageIdForPath fails (e.g. realpath fails), the catch swallows the error and imageId remains set to the original target string, causing rm to silently do nothing.
code_excerpt: try { imageId = await imageIdForPath(target); } catch {}
evidence: cli.ts:302 shows an empty `catch {}` around imageIdForPath. If realpath fails (path doesn't exist), imageId remains as the raw path string, which then fails the per-image lock acquisition or removeImage silently returns no-op. The user sees '0 dirs, 0 levels removed' with no error message explaining that the path was not found.

title: waitForReadLocksToClear polls at 50-100ms fixed interval with no exponential backoff
description: Read-lock-drain loop polls every 50-100ms generating O(readers) stat/readFile calls per cycle on slow FS.
code_excerpt: await sleep(50 + Math.random() * 50);
evidence: lock.ts shows fixed 50-100ms polling interval. For a gallery with many concurrent validate readers on a network filesystem, this generates excessive I/O. Exponential backoff capped at ~500ms would reduce load without material impact on worst-case wait time.

title: measureConvergenceProfile bytesPushed ordering question — same as logic-1
description: This finding questions the event ordering for bytesPushed vs progress events — essentially asking whether logic-1 is a real bug.
code_excerpt:
bytesPushed += chunk.length;
      await Promise.resolve(decoder.push(chunk));
evidence: This is a re-examination of logic-1 from a different angle. The pre-increment is confirmed real in logic-1. This finding adds no new information — it is subsumed by logic-1's confirmation. The event ordering is deterministic: progress events fire synchronously within decoder.push() before the await resolves, and bytesPushed is already incremented before push is called.

title: plan! non-null assertion on line 506 can throw if buildFallbackPlan unexpectedly returns null-like
description: applyIngestPlan(plan!, ...) uses a non-null assertion. plan is initialized to null and set in all branches, but a missed branch could produce a TypeError.
code_excerpt: await applyIngestPlan(plan!, backends, opts);
evidence: ingest.ts:471 initializes plan as null. Every code path either assigns plan or throws before reaching line 506. The TypeScript type system and the code structure guarantee plan is assigned; the non-null assertion is defensive notation, not a latent null-dereference risk. The finder's self-reported confidence is 'low' and the analysis correctly notes buildFallbackPlan never returns null.

title: createJxlBackend methods have no timeout on WASM calls — a hung WASM encoder will block indefinitely
description: All JxlBackend methods call into WASM without a timeout guard. WASM push() is synchronous and holds the event loop.
evidence: A synchronous WASM infinite loop would block Node's event loop entirely, preventing the per-image timeout Promise.race from ever resolving. The ingestImage timeout at ingest.ts:514 would therefore be ineffective against a truly hung WASM call. A worker-level watchdog via worker_threads is the correct mitigation, consistent with the CLAUDE.md description of synchronous WASM behavior.

title: IngestResult.outcome is typed 'written'|'skipped' but ingestImage returns 'written' for dry-run even when no write occurred
description: Duplicate of logic-14. dry-run path returns outcome:'written', inflating result.written count.
code_excerpt: return { outcome: 'written', plan: plan!, degraded: usedFallback || undefined };
evidence: Duplicate confirmation of logic-14. ingest.ts:503 returns outcome:'written' in dryRun branch. ingest.ts:724 unconditionally increments result.written for outcome==='written', including dry-runs.

title: tiling-ingest tests use imageIdForPath synchronously but it is async, causing always-undefined imageId
description: Duplicate of logic-4/errors-4.
code_excerpt: const imageId = imageIdForPath(master);  // missing await
evidence: Duplicate of logic-4. Confirmed.

title: tiling-ingest.test.ts expects ingestImage to return the string 'written' but IngestResult is an object
description: Duplicate of errors-6.
code_excerpt: expect(await ingestImage(master, backends, { outDir: out })).toBe('written');
evidence: Duplicate of errors-6. Confirmed.

title: planShard returns empty array silently for out-of-range i or n<=0 instead of throwing as the test asserts
description: Duplicate of logic-3.
evidence: Duplicate of logic-3. shard.ts:28-29 confirmed. The test at shard.test.ts:17-20 asserts throws for all three guard conditions, but the implementation returns silently.

title: Tier-5 stub buildFallbackPlan builds an IngestPlan whose manifest.format field accepts values outside MasterFormat union
description: The Tier-5 stub sets `format: detected as any` where detected may be 'unknown'. MasterFormat type does not include 'unknown', causing a type mismatch suppressed by `as any`.
code_excerpt: master: { name: identity.masterName, format: detected as any, mtimeMs: identity.mtimeMs },
evidence: schema.ts:55 shows masterInfoSchema includes 'unknown' as a valid format. The TypeScript type MasterFormat in backends.ts does not include 'unknown'. The `as any` cast suppresses this. A caller receiving a manifest with format:'unknown' cannot assign it to MasterFormat without another cast. The schema and type are inconsistent — schema.ts is the ground truth and MasterFormat should be extended to include 'unknown'.

title: readCheckpoint parses checkpoint JSON without schema validation, silently returning malformed state
description: readCheckpoint does `JSON.parse(txt) as CheckpointState` with no Zod validation. A truncated or corrupt checkpoint returns partial state whose .completed/.failed/.inFlight arrays are undefined, causing TypeError on .map()/.filter() calls.
code_excerpt: return JSON.parse(txt) as CheckpointState;  // no validation
evidence: checkpoint.ts:68 confirms bare JSON.parse with type cast. If the checkpoint file is truncated (e.g. crash during write), JSON.parse may return an object missing .completed, .failed, or .inFlight arrays. Callers in ingestBatch use `(checkpoint.completed || [])` style guards in some places but direct array access in others. A Zod schema parse with fallback-to-null on failure would be more robust.

title: CliEvent type declares 'image-done' event but cli.ts emits 'image-end', creating a reader/writer mismatch
description: CliEvent union at schema.ts:153 declares `type: 'image-done'` but ingest.ts:731 and cli.ts:895 emit `'image-end'`.
code_excerpt:
| { type: 'image-done'; ... }  // declared
tel?.event?.('image-end', ...);  // actual emission
evidence: schema.ts:153 declares `{ type: 'image-done'; ... }`. ingest.ts:731 emits `'image-end'`. cli.ts:895 emits `'image-end'`. Any consumer discriminating on `type === 'image-done'` receives no events. The declared CliEvent contract is a dead letter — the correct type string is `'image-end'`.

title: migrateSchema with targetVersion=4 writes schema:4 but the raw transform adds no v4-specific fields, producing invalid manifests that may fail validation
description: The CLI's --migrate-schema path does parseInt without validating against SUPPORTED_SCHEMA_TARGETS, so --migrate-schema 99 is only caught inside migrateSchema.
code_excerpt: const schemaTarget = parsed['migrate-schema'] ? (parseInt(parsed['migrate-schema'] as string, 10) as any) : 2;
evidence: cli.ts:328 confirms parseInt with `as any` cast and no validation against SUPPORTED_SCHEMA_TARGETS before calling migrateSchema. An unsupported target like 99 would reach migrateSchema which returns an error record rather than throwing, but the CLI does not check that return value for errors, potentially reporting success for an unsupported migration.

title: toEntry sets bytes=level.data.length but after dry-run truncation data becomes Uint8Array(0), recording 0 bytes in the manifest entry
description: In dry-run mode, level data is zeroed after toEntry is called, so printPlanHuman shows 0.0 KB for all levels.
evidence: ingest.ts:498-503 shows data is zeroed after `computeIngestPlan` returns — and `toEntry` is called inside computeIngestPlan before the data zeroing. The plan.entries array is built with correct bytes values before zeroing. The display issue only affects code that calls toEntry after zeroing, which would be a bug in the caller, not in this path. The manifest written to disk (in non-dry-run runs) uses pre-zeroing entries and is correct.

title: measureConvergenceProfile skips levels with max(w,h) < 1024 but QualityCurvePoint schema requires bytes > 0 while bytesPushed starts at 0
description: If a progress event fires before any chunk is pushed, bytesPushed=0 and the curve point would fail schema validation with z.number().int().positive().
code_excerpt: bytes: z.number().int().positive(),  // rejects 0
evidence: The progress event fires inside `decoder.push(chunk)` only after data has been pushed — it cannot fire before any chunk is pushed. Since bytesPushed is pre-incremented (logic-1 bug), it is at minimum chunk.length > 0 when the first progress event fires. A bytes=0 point is not reachable in practice.

title: CliEvent union type is declared but never validated against actual emission at test or runtime boundaries
description: The CliEvent type documents the --json event stream contract but has no Zod schema and no test validates actual emission against it.
code_excerpt: export type CliEvent = ...;  // TypeScript type only, no Zod schema
evidence: contracts-7 proves this gap is real — the 'image-done' vs 'image-end' mismatch has existed without being caught. There is no Zod schema for CliEvent and no test that captures --json stdout and validates event shapes. Adding a Zod CliEventSchema and a single integration test that parses emitted events would have caught contracts-7.

title: manifestSchema discriminated union has no forward-compatibility sentinel for schema versions > 4
description: A manifest with schema:5 written by a newer tool version will throw a cryptic Zod union failure rather than a clear 'unsupported schema version' error.
code_excerpt: export const manifestSchema = z.discriminatedUnion('schema', [v1, v2base, v4base]);
evidence: schema.ts:91 confirms the discriminated union only handles 1, 2, 4. Any future schema version produces a Zod error message 'Invalid discriminator value. Expected 1 | 2 | 4' which is confusing. The producedBy.version major-version guard is bypassed since Zod throws before reaching it.

title: ingestBatch has no idempotency guard when called concurrently with same outDir and overlapping file sets
description: Multiple concurrent ingestBatch invocations on the same outDir with overlapping files will each re-ingest shared images, wasting work.
code_excerpt:
const batchId = checkpoint?.batchId || randomUUID();
// no cross-process dedup
evidence: The per-image write lock (acquireImageWriteLock) serializes concurrent ingests of the same image — the second process will block and then detect the image is already written via the mtime+stat check in ingestImage, returning 'skipped'. This is effectively idempotent already. The concern about wasted work is real but the correctness impact is low.

title: Uint8ClampedArray.from(finalPixels) copies full master pixel buffer on every profileConvergenceCurve call
description: refImg is built via Uint8ClampedArray.from(finalPixels) copying the entire final-frame RGBA buffer. finalPixels itself is already a copy from decodeFinal. Double copy for large images.
code_excerpt: const refImg = ssimFn ? { data: Uint8ClampedArray.from(finalPixels), width: useW, height: useH } : null;
evidence: backends.ts:285-291 confirms finalPixels from decodeFinal and then Uint8ClampedArray.from(finalPixels) for refImg. For a 12MP RGBA image (~50MB), this is a second full-buffer copy allocated per level profiling call. Since ssim.js only reads the data array, constructing refImg directly from the original ArrayBuffer without the Uint8Array intermediate would eliminate one copy.

title: Uint8ClampedArray.from(px) allocates a full-frame copy on every progressive pass for SSIM
description: Inside the progressive decode event loop (called once per JXL pass, ~10-20 times per level), Uint8ClampedArray.from(px) copies the full pass pixel buffer (~50MB) per pass.
code_excerpt: const img1 = { data: Uint8ClampedArray.from(px), width: useW, height: useH };
evidence: backends.ts:327 shows Uint8ClampedArray.from(px) inside the progress event handler. With 10-20 progressive passes at 50MB each, this generates 500MB-1GB of transient GC pressure per level profiling call. A reusable scratch Uint8ClampedArray pre-allocated to max expected size and filled with .set() would eliminate all these allocations.

title: probeOrientation called twice for JPG path: once in computeIngestPlan and once in buildFallbackPlan
description: probeOrientation dynamically imports exifr and parses EXIF data. Called twice for JPG paths.
code_excerpt: if (await probeOrientation(bytes) === "baked") {
evidence: The finding describes a pattern in computeIngestPlan and buildFallbackPlan where probeOrientation is called on similar or identical bytes. The dynamic import of exifr is cached after first call, so the overhead is primarily the EXIF parse itself. This is a real but minor redundancy since orientation is fast to extract.

title: removeOrphans stat()s each orphan candidate serially inside a sequential for-loop
description: The orphan scan loop stat()s and unlink()s level files serially. With many orphans this is slow.
evidence: ingest.ts:1015-1028 confirms serial stat + unlink in a for-await loop. For a large gallery post-failure with many orphan levels, this is a significant wall-time bottleneck. Wrapping with pMapLimit(16) would provide meaningful improvement consistent with how manifest scanning is already bounded.

title: removeOrphans step 3 (empty image dirs) calls fileExists() serially for each image id
description: The empty-image-dirs loop calls fileExists + rm serially for each image ID.
evidence: ingest.ts:1032-1044 confirms serial fileExists + rm loop over all image IDs. For galleries with thousands of images this is slow. pMapLimit would provide the same improvement as perf-4.

title: rebuildIndex calls fileExists() then readFile() per image — two stat/open syscalls instead of one
description: fileExists (access()) followed by readFile on the same path is redundant. readFileOrNull already exists in the codebase.
code_excerpt:
if (!(await fileExists(manifestPath))) return;
let manifest = parseManifest(await readFile(manifestPath, "utf8"));
evidence: ingest.ts:941-944 confirms the double-syscall pattern. readFileOrNull is already used elsewhere (validate.ts:55) and would collapse this to one call, eliminate the TOCTOU window between access and open, and reduce syscall count by ~50% for rebuildIndex on large galleries.

title: f.replace(/\.jxl$/, "") uses a regex inside the orphan-scan loop
description: Should use f.slice(0, -4) consistently with validate.ts, slightly more efficient.
code_excerpt: const h = f.replace(/\.jxl$/, "");
evidence: ingest.ts:1017 uses regex replace while validate.ts:105 uses f.slice(0, -4) for the identical pattern. V8 caches literal regexes so the performance delta is small, but the inconsistency is real and slice is marginally more efficient.

title: index.json written with JSON.stringify(index, null, 2) — pretty-printed for a machine-read file
description: Gallery index pretty-printed adds ~20-30% size overhead. Machine-read file should use compact serialization.
code_excerpt: await withEbusyRetry(() => writeFile(tmp, JSON.stringify(index, null, 2)), "write-index-tmp");
evidence: ingest.ts:964 confirms `JSON.stringify(index, null, 2)`. For 10k images the index could be 1-1.5MB; pretty-printing adds ~20-30% to both write and subsequent read I/O. Compact serialization is consistent with checkpoint.ts:76 which uses `JSON.stringify(state)` without indentation.

title: cpState.completed.reduce() on the entire completed array at batch end — O(N) over all prior-run entries
description: Duplicate of logic-6's performance aspect. totalStagedBytes reduce walks all entries including prior-run entries.
code_excerpt: const s = cpState.completed.reduce((sum: number, c: any) => sum + (c.stagedBytes || 0), 0);
evidence: Duplicate of logic-6 (correctness aspect). ingest.ts:756 and :926 both use unfiltered reduce. The performance concern is that for --resume with a large checkpoint, this walks thousands of prior-run entries. Using a running sum (incremented only in the per-image success handler) would be O(1) and also fix the logic-6 inflation bug.

title: Object spread on opts for every dispatched worker job copies the entire IngestOptions record
description: Each worker job creates `{ ...opts, statMap: undefined, ... }`, copying all of IngestOptions per dispatch.
code_excerpt: const jobOpts: any = { ...opts, statMap: undefined, statEntry: ..., idMap: undefined, imageId: preId };
evidence: ingest.ts:881 confirms the spread on every dispatch. With high concurrency and many images, each spread creates a fresh object copy. The IngestOptions object may carry large fields (statMap can have thousands of entries) that are stripped via `statMap: undefined` only in the spread output, but the spread itself still reads all keys. A purpose-built minimal job type would reduce allocator and postMessage serialization overhead.

title: collectInputs performs a second parallel stat() on every candidate after already obtaining isDirectory() via withFileTypes
description: stat() is issued on all candidates to get mtime/size, even for files that may be skipped by the downstream mtime check.
evidence: cli.ts:52-55 confirms stat() on all candidates. The pre-collection of stat data enables the statMap optimization that avoids re-stating inside ingestImage. This is a deliberate design tradeoff for performance. The finding is a genuine opportunity but marked complex because deferring stats would require re-architecting the statMap pattern.

title: No per-level profiling breakdowns for the two-decode cost of profileConvergenceCurve
description: profileConvergence emits a single stage event with no breakdown of decodeFinal vs progressive decode vs per-pass metric cost.
code_excerpt: tel?.stage?.("profile-convergence", { w, h, kind: "cutoff", ms, converged: !!prof?.convergedByteEnd });
evidence: backends.ts confirms the single-aggregate stage event. Without a breakdown, operators cannot distinguish whether convergence profiling time is dominated by decodeFinal, progressive decode, or SSIM/Butteraugli computation per pass. This information would directly inform whether caching the final-ref decode across levels would provide meaningful savings.

title: No ingest throughput benchmark; performance regressions in encode/decode paths are invisible until production
description: The test suite exercises correctness but does not measure wall-time or throughput for the pyramid-ingest Node.js pipeline.
code_excerpt: // No benchmark file exists under packages/pyramid-ingest/
evidence: No benchmark file found under packages/pyramid-ingest/ (confirmed by Glob). The codebase has benchmark infrastructure under benchmark/ for WASM encode but nothing for the full ingest pipeline. This is a real CI gap that would catch performance regressions in pMapLimit tuning, existingLevels Set construction, and serial I/O patterns before they reach production.

title: --explain with imageId stat()s each level file serially to count on-disk presence
description: The --explain path stat()s each level file serially. A single readdir + Set lookup would be O(1) total instead of O(levels) serial round-trips.
evidence: cli.ts:173-179 confirms serial stat per level for --explain. With 5 levels this is 5 round-trips vs 1 readdir + Set.has(). For the --explain diagnostic command the impact is minimal (5 stats vs 1 readdir on a typically small manifest), but the inconsistency with the existingLevels Set pattern used in writeLevelFiles is worth fixing.
